title: Dopamine Reward Circuit

summary.beginner: Dopamine is less about pleasure and more about anticipation and wanting — it's the 'I want to do that again' signal, not the 'that feels good' signal. The analogy: dopamine is the carrot being dangled, not the reward of eating it. A concrete example: the excitement you feel checking your phone for a message — the dopamine rush happens in the moment of checking, not necessarily from the content you find.
summary.university: The mesolimbic dopamine pathway projects from ventral tegmental area (VTA) to nucleus accumbens (NAc), amygdala, and hippocampus. Dopamine release encodes reward prediction errors (RPE): phasic bursts for better-than-expected outcomes, pauses for worse-than-expected. D1-MSN activation in NAc core strengthens goal-directed behaviour via AMPA receptor trafficking (LTP); D2-MSNs in NAc shell mediate suppression of competing responses. Within behavioural neuroscience and addiction biology, the mesolimbic circuit is the substrate of incentive salience — 'wanting' rather than 'liking.'
summary.research: Berridge & Robinson's incentive salience hypothesis (1998) dissociates dopamine's role in 'wanting' (incentive motivation, dopamine-dependent) from 'liking' (hedonic impact, opioid-dependent) — dopamine-depleted rats stop seeking food but still show pleasure responses when food is given. Computationally, dopamine implements the temporal difference RPE signal δ(t) = r(t) + γV(s') − V(s) driving Q-learning-style value updates. An open question is whether tonic versus phasic dopamine have distinct computational roles — tonic dopamine may set opportunity cost thresholds (the baseline worth of acting), while phasic dopamine drives specific learning updates, with disrupted tonic tone underlying motivational deficits in depression.

real_life_examples: The rush of excitement before opening a gift
Social media notification dopamine loops
Runner's high from sustained aerobic exercise
Compulsive gambling driven by variable reward schedules

title: Amygdala Fear Response

summary.beginner: The amygdala is your brain's smoke detector — it fires before you even consciously register a threat, triggering fear responses faster than thought. The analogy: a smoke detector doesn't wait for you to smell the smoke; it blares at the first sign. A concrete example: jumping backwards before consciously processing that the stick on the path is just a stick — your amygdala reacted to a snake-like shape before your cortex had time to identify it.
summary.university: The basolateral amygdala (BLA) receives sensory input via two routes: a fast subcortical thalamo-amygdala path (low-resolution, fast) and a slower cortical path (high-resolution, slow). BLA appraises emotional significance and projects to the central nucleus (CeA), which drives fear expression: HPA axis activation via hypothalamus, autonomic arousal via brainstem, and behavioural freezing via periaqueductal grey. Fear conditioning — a key model in affective neuroscience — depends on NMDA-receptor-dependent LTP at BLA synapses.
summary.research: LeDoux's work (1994–2000) established the dual-route model of threat appraisal and the specific role of BLA LTP in fear conditioning. Extinction — the suppression of conditioned fear — requires active vmPFC (infralimbic cortex) inhibitory projections to BLA interneurons; PTSD may reflect impaired infralimbic extinction recall rather than failure of extinction acquisition, formalised computationally as a failure of contextual gating of extinction memories. An open question is whether human conscious fear experience requires amygdala activity — SM, a patient with bilateral amygdala lesions, shows absent fear responses in most conditions but not all, suggesting distributed substrates for emotional experience beyond the amygdala.

real_life_examples: Jumping at a sudden loud noise
Heart racing when nearly in a car accident
Phobias from single traumatic experiences
PTSD flashback triggered by a smell

title: Prefrontal Cortex Executive Function

summary.beginner: Think of the prefrontal cortex as your brain's CEO — it sits behind your forehead and decides what to do, when to do it, and what to ignore. A concrete example: it's what stops you from blurting out something rude in a meeting, overriding the impulse your emotional brain generated.
summary.university: The dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC, BA9/46) supports working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control via persistent recurrent neural firing in layer-III pyramidal circuits. It exerts top-down regulation over the amygdala and striatum through glutamatergic cortico-limbic projections. This sits within cognitive neuroscience and neuropsychology, with maturation continuing through the mid-20s — the neural explanation for adolescent impulsivity and risk-taking.
summary.research: dlPFC sustains working-memory representations through NMDA-NR2B × D1 receptor co-activation; an inverted-U dose-response means both too little and too much catecholamine (norepinephrine via α1-AR, dopamine via D2R) degrades signal-to-noise. Goldman-Rakic's foundational work (1995) established the dlPFC as the substrate of working memory in primates; Arnsten extended this to the pharmacological tuning of prefrontal circuits. An open question is whether TMS-induced dlPFC modulation in depression works by directly restoring sgACC inhibition or via secondary network reorganisation.

real_life_examples: Resisting dessert while on a diet
Planning a complex project step-by-step
Staying calm during an argument
Teenagers taking risks that adults avoid

title: Hippocampal Memory Consolidation

summary.beginner: The hippocampus is the brain's memory stamp — it takes new experiences and encodes them into a form that can later be stored across the cortex. The analogy: the hippocampus is a film development lab; raw experience is the exposed film, and the lab processes it into permanent photographs during sleep. A concrete example: studying before sleep produces significantly better recall the next day than studying before an equivalent period of wakefulness — the sleep consolidation process is responsible.
summary.university: The hippocampus performs rapid, high-capacity encoding of episodic memories via pattern separation in the dentate gyrus (DG — storing distinct representations of similar events) and pattern completion in CA3 (retrieving complete memories from partial cues). Systems consolidation transfers memories to distributed neocortical storage during NREM sleep via sharp-wave ripple (SWR) sequences in CA1 that trigger thalamic spindles and neocortical slow oscillations. This falls within cognitive neuroscience and systems memory research.
summary.research: The Complementary Learning Systems (CLS) framework (McClelland, McNaughton & O'Reilly, 1995) formalises the division: hippocampus uses fast one-shot learning to encode specific episodes; neocortex uses slow statistical learning to extract structure. Mathematically, hippocampal pattern separation is implemented by sparse orthogonal coding in DG, reducing interference between similar memories. An open question is the role of adult neurogenesis in DG: new neurons produced in adult dentate gyrus (in rodents robustly, in humans controversially) are proposed to support pattern separation by providing 'blank' unbiased encoders — but whether adult human hippocampal neurogenesis is sufficient for functional significance remains actively debated.

real_life_examples: Remembering where you parked your car
Sleep improving exam performance
H.M.'s inability to form new memories after hippocampal removal
Déjà vu from pattern completion misfires

title: Confirmation Bias

summary.beginner: Confirmation bias is the brain's tendency to act like a defence attorney rather than a detective — gathering evidence that supports the case you've already decided, ignoring everything else. A concrete everyday example: a sports fan who only notices the referee's bad calls against their own team while missing equally bad calls against the other side.
summary.university: Confirmation bias operates across three distinct stages: selective information search (preferentially querying sources likely to confirm), biased interpretation (ambiguous evidence is read as supporting prior belief), and selective memory recall (confirming instances are better encoded and retrieved). The anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) generates conflict signals for disconfirming information, but motivated reasoning circuits — involving vmPFC and amygdala — can suppress these signals when beliefs are identity-relevant. Within social cognition and behavioural economics, this is studied as motivated reasoning and myside bias.
summary.research: Bayesian models frame confirmation bias as a failure of prior-updating: the posterior update weight α given to new evidence is scaled downward by belief strength, producing asymmetric evidence integration. Klayman & Ha (1987) re-analysed Wason's 2-4-6 task, arguing the observed 'bias' reflects a positive test strategy that is ecologically rational under most real-world hypothesis spaces. An open question is whether confirmation bias is reducible by System 2 engagement or whether it reflects deeper motivated belief protection that deliberative reasoning cannot access — evidence from IQ-matched samples suggests the latter.

real_life_examples: Only reading news sources that match your politics
A manager who only notices evidence that their favourite employee is performing well
Investors holding losing stocks, seeking confirming data
Medical misdiagnosis from premature closure

title: Anchoring Bias

summary.beginner: Anchoring is the brain's tendency to lock onto the first number it encounters and use it as a reference for all subsequent judgements, even when that number is completely irrelevant. The classic example: in experiments where participants spin a wheel to get a random number (10 or 65) before estimating what percentage of African countries are in the UN, those who got 65 gave much higher estimates — a wheel spin affecting a geography judgement.
summary.university: Two mechanisms drive anchoring: selective accessibility (anchors prime consistent knowledge, making anchor-supporting information more accessible, via PFC and striatal semantic networks) and insufficient adjustment (people start at the anchor and adjust, but stop adjusting too early — reflecting the confirmatory nature of the adjustment process). The caudate nucleus encodes reference points; vmPFC computes value relative to that anchor. Within behavioural economics and social cognition, anchoring distorts price perception, salary negotiations, legal sentencing, and clinical estimates.
summary.research: Strack & Mussweiler's confirmatory hypothesis-testing model (1997) explains anchoring as anchor-consistent knowledge activation elevating the plausibility of anchor-consistent assessments. Epley & Gilovich (2006) dissociate knowledge-based anchoring (semantic priming) from adjustment-based anchoring; only the latter is reduced by increased motivation and cognitive resources. An open question is whether anchoring is domain-general (one mechanism) or whether numerical anchoring, social anchoring, and temporal anchoring involve distinct neural substrates — neuroimaging evidence remains preliminary.

real_life_examples: MSRP pricing making a 'sale' price feel like a deal
Salary negotiation outcomes skewed by who names a number first
Arbitrary spin-the-wheel numbers influencing charity donations
Court sentences anchored by prosecution's initial demand

title: Loss Aversion

summary.beginner: Losses feel roughly twice as painful as equivalent gains feel pleasurable — losing £50 hurts more than finding £50 feels good. The analogy: the emotional scales are rigged in favour of losses. The concrete example: people will work much harder to avoid losing a £10 note they already have than to gain a £10 note they don't yet have — identical stakes, radically different effort.
summary.university: Kahneman & Tversky's (1979) Prospect Theory formalises loss aversion via an S-shaped value function steeper in the loss domain: v(x) = x^α for gains, v(x) = −λ(−x)^β for losses, with λ ≈ 2.25 empirically. Neural correlates: insula and amygdala activation scales with anticipated loss magnitude; vmPFC tracks expected value; striatal BOLD tracks gains. Individual differences in λ correlate with insula-vmPFC functional connectivity. Within behavioural economics and neuroeconomics, loss aversion explains status quo bias, the endowment effect, and the disposition effect in investing.
summary.research: Tom et al. (2007) demonstrated that BOLD signals in striatum and vmPFC asymmetrically encode gains and losses — the slope of neural response to losses is steeper than to gains — and this neural asymmetry coefficient predicts individual behavioural loss aversion (λ). Recent meta-analyses (Yechiam, 2019) question the universality of λ ≈ 2: loss aversion magnitude varies substantially with elicitation method, stakes, and domain, with some suggesting attention rather than value asymmetry as the primary driver. An open question is whether loss aversion is a basic feature of value representation or an emergent property of the comparison process — and whether mindfulness training (reducing reactive avoidance) can systematically attenuate loss aversion by decoupling affective response from choice.

real_life_examples: Holding a losing stock to avoid realising the loss
Endowment effect: valuing owned items more than identical unowned ones
Insurance over-purchasing beyond rational expected value
Not quitting a bad job due to loss of seniority

title: Cognitive Dissonance

summary.beginner: Cognitive dissonance is the mental discomfort you feel when your actions contradict your beliefs — and the uncomfortable mental gymnastics your mind performs to resolve that tension. A classic everyday example: a smoker who knows smoking is lethal but continues; to reduce the discomfort, the brain generates rationalisations ('I could get hit by a bus tomorrow anyway') rather than changing the behaviour.
summary.university: Festinger (1957) proposed that inconsistent cognitions generate aversive psychological arousal motivating reduction via: attitude change, adding new consonant cognitions, trivialisation, or behavioural change. Neural signatures involve ACC conflict detection and anterior insula encoding the aversive arousal of inconsistency. Within social psychology and cognitive neuroscience, dissonance is studied via induced compliance, free-choice, and effort-justification paradigms — all producing attitude shift toward consistency.
summary.research: fMRI studies using free-choice and induced-compliance paradigms (van Veen et al., 2009) reveal that ACC-anterior insula coupling encodes dissonance magnitude, and its degree predicts the subsequent attitude shift used to reduce dissonance. Predictive processing frameworks reframe dissonance as a self-model prediction error: behaviour (output) conflicts with the generative model of self (prior belief), and attitude change minimises free energy by updating the prior rather than changing behaviour. An open question is whether cognitive dissonance is a universal phenomenon or substantially modulated by cultural orientation — collectivist-culture studies show attenuated dissonance in choice-relevant domains, suggesting self-concept architecture moderates dissonance magnitude.

real_life_examples: Smokers convincing themselves 'it won't happen to me'
Post-purchase rationalisation of an expensive item
Cult members doubling down after failed prophecy
'Hazing creates loyalty' justification among fraternity members

title: Working Memory

summary.beginner: Working memory is the brain's mental sticky note — it holds a small amount of information active for immediate use. The analogy: RAM in a computer versus hard drive storage. Working memory is RAM: fast, limited, constantly in use. A concrete example: holding a sequence of directions in mind while driving through an unfamiliar area — you're actively rehearsing 'left, then roundabout, then second exit' to keep it accessible.
summary.university: Baddeley's (1974, 2000) multi-component model comprises the central executive (dlPFC — attention and coordination), phonological loop (left inferior frontal/parietal — verbal rehearsal), visuospatial sketchpad (right parietal/occipital — spatial information), and episodic buffer (binding across modalities). Capacity is ~4 chunks (Cowan, 2001) — not 7±2, which measures chunks not items. Persistent neural firing in dlPFC layer III sustains representations via recurrent excitation; parietal cortex maintains spatial information. Within cognitive psychology, working memory capacity is the single strongest predictor of fluid intelligence and academic achievement.
summary.research: Goldman-Rakic (1995) established dlPFC as the substrate of working memory via single-unit recordings in monkeys showing sustained firing for remembered cues across delay periods. The sustained activity is maintained by NMDA-NR2B × D1 receptor co-activation, following an inverted-U dose-response to catecholamines. Theta-gamma coupling — theta phase (from hippocampus/PFC) carrying multiple gamma cycles (each encoding one item) — is proposed as the oscillatory code for multi-item working memory (Lisman & Jensen, 2013). An open question is whether working memory capacity limits reflect a fixed structural constraint (interference between representations) or a flexible resource-allocation limit — the sensory recruitment hypothesis proposes capacity can be extended by recruiting posterior sensory cortex as additional storage.

real_life_examples: Holding a phone number in mind while dialling
Mental arithmetic (47 × 8)
Following multi-step verbal directions
Understanding complex nested sentences

title: Cortisol & Stress Response

summary.beginner: Cortisol is the body's stress mobiliser — a hormone that prepares you to handle a threat by raising blood sugar, sharpening focus, and suppressing non-urgent functions like digestion and immunity. The analogy: cortisol is like an emergency generator that powers the essentials when the main grid fails. A concrete example: the exam panic that causes your mind to go blank is cortisol levels spiking so high that hippocampal memory retrieval is actively impaired.
summary.university: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis orchestrates the response: hypothalamic CRH → pituitary ACTH → adrenal cortisol secretion. Cortisol binds glucocorticoid (GR) and mineralocorticoid (MR) receptors: MR is occupied at baseline, GR engages under peak stress. Cortisol enhances amygdala activity (amplifying threat appraisal) while impairing hippocampal LTP (reducing new learning during acute stress). Negative feedback via hippocampal and PFC GRs normally terminates the response. Within neuroendocrinology and clinical psychology, HPA axis dysregulation underlies depression, PTSD, and metabolic syndrome.
summary.research: McEwen's allostatic load framework (1998) formalises cumulative stress burden as wear-and-tear on regulatory systems, with chronic HPA hyperactivation causing hippocampal CA3 dendritic atrophy via glutamate excitotoxicity and reduced BDNF. Epigenetically, early-life adversity methylates NR3C1 (glucocorticoid receptor gene) via NGFI-A binding, reducing GR expression and impairing negative feedback — a mechanism described by Meaney's rat-pup licking studies and extended to humans via Sudbrack-Oliveira et al. An open question is whether cortisol-mediated hippocampal atrophy in depression is reversible with antidepressant treatment — meta-analyses suggest partial volume recovery with successful treatment, but the cellular mechanisms (neurogenesis vs. dendritic regrowth) are unresolved.

real_life_examples: Exam panic causing memory blanks
Chronic work stress leading to burnout
Public speaking triggering physical symptoms
Post-traumatic cortisol dysregulation

title: Default Mode Network

summary.beginner: The Default Mode Network is your brain's 'screensaver' — the activity that kicks in the moment you stop focusing on a task. Think of it like a browser returning to a home page: your mind wanders to yourself, other people, or the future. The classic example is a brilliant idea arriving in the shower when you weren't consciously thinking about it.
summary.university: The DMN comprises medial PFC (mPFC), posterior cingulate cortex (PCC), angular gyrus, and hippocampal formation. It is functionally anti-correlated with task-positive frontoparietal networks, indexed in fMRI by BOLD signal. Within cognitive neuroscience, DMN supports autobiographical memory retrieval, prospective simulation, theory of mind, and self-referential processing — and is among the earliest networks disrupted in Alzheimer's disease.
summary.research: DMN connectivity emerges from correlated slow BOLD fluctuations (<0.1 Hz); Buckner, Andrews-Hanna & Schacter (2008) synthesised its core architecture and proposed it serves 'scene construction.' Computationally, the PCC acts as a Bayesian hub integrating prior knowledge (hippocampus) with current context (mPFC) to support predictive coding of self-relevant events. An open question is whether default-mode rumination in depression represents excessive self-referential simulation or a failure of anti-correlated task-network suppression.

real_life_examples: Daydreaming during a boring meeting
Suddenly solving a problem in the shower
Imagining a future conversation
Mind-wandering improving long-term creative insight

title: Reward Prediction Error

summary.beginner: Reward prediction error is the brain's surprise detector for outcomes — when something is better than expected, you get a dopamine burst; when worse than expected, dopamine dips. The analogy: imagine a boss who gives no feedback when you perform as expected, enthusiastically praises you for exceeding targets, and visibly withdraws approval when you fall short — that's exactly how your VTA dopamine neurons behave, training the rest of your brain through surprise.
summary.university: RPE is formalised as δ = r − V(s): the difference between received reward (r) and predicted value V(s). VTA dopamine neurons encode this signal phasically: unexpected rewards → burst firing, expected rewards → no change, expected-reward omission → pause in firing. This RPE signal drives temporal difference (TD) reinforcement learning, updating value estimates V(s) by a learning rate α. Within computational neuroscience and psychiatry, disrupted RPE signalling is implicated in addiction (sensitised D1-MSN responses), schizophrenia (aberrant salience), and depression (blunted positive RPE).
summary.research: Schultz, Dayan & Montague (1997) provided the landmark demonstration that VTA dopamine neurons track RPE with millisecond precision, shifting firing from US (unconditioned stimulus) to CS (conditioned stimulus) across learning — matching the TD-learning prediction exactly. Optogenetic tagging studies confirm that dopamine neuron bursts, not GABA or glutamate co-release, carry the RPE signal to NAc. An open question is whether serotonin encodes negative prediction errors in a complementary system: Matias et al. (2017) found dorsal raphe serotonin neurons encode the negative RPE (worse than expected), but this dissociation's robustness and universality remain debated.

real_life_examples: Variable ratio slot machine reinforcement maximising engagement
Disappointment when a usually delicious meal is average
Excitement from an unexpected compliment
Stock market surprises driving stronger emotional reactions than expected moves

title: Serotonin & Mood Regulation

summary.beginner: Serotonin is often called the 'wellbeing neurotransmitter,' but a better analogy is a volume dial on negative emotion — serotonin doesn't make you euphoric, it damps down anxiety, irritability, and distress, keeping mood stable. A concrete example: seasonal affective disorder (SAD) in winter, when reduced sunlight lowers serotonin synthesis, directly producing low mood, increased sleep, and carbohydrate craving.
summary.university: Serotonergic neurons in the dorsal and median raphe nuclei project widely to limbic structures (amygdala, hippocampus), PFC, striatum, and hypothalamus. Serotonin acts at 14+ receptor subtypes: 5-HT1A autoreceptors on raphe neurons regulate firing rate; 5-HT2A on cortical pyramidal neurons modulate excitability and are the target of psychedelic drugs. SSRIs block the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin — though the therapeutic lag (2–4 weeks) suggests mood improvement depends on downstream plasticity changes rather than acute serotonin increases. Within psychopharmacology and affective neuroscience, the monoamine hypothesis of depression is now considered oversimplified.
summary.research: Carhart-Harris & Nutt (2017) reframed the serotonin–depression relationship: serotonin's primary function may be promoting synaptic and behavioural flexibility rather than mood per se — a 'REBUS' (Relaxed Beliefs Under Psychedelics) model where 5-HT2A agonism flattens the precision-weighting of priors, enabling new learning. Tryptophan depletion — dietary removal of the serotonin precursor — reliably provokes depression relapse in remitted patients but not in naïve volunteers, confirming serotonin modulates mood vulnerability rather than causing it directly. An open question is whether SSRIs' therapeutic benefit operates primarily via 5-HT1A-mediated hippocampal neurogenesis (fluoxetine promotes DG neurogenesis, which is required for its antidepressant effect in rodents) or via frontolimbic connectivity normalisation — these mechanisms may coexist but their relative contribution remains debated.

real_life_examples: Winter depression (SAD) from reduced sunlight
Exercise improving mood via serotonin and endorphins
Antidepressant mechanisms of SSRIs
Psilocybin's mood effects via 5-HT2A agonism

title: Availability Heuristic

summary.beginner: The availability heuristic is the shortcut of judging likelihood by how easily an example pops into mind — and vividness fools you. The everyday analogy: after seeing a shark attack story on the news, the ocean suddenly feels dangerous, even though the statistical risk hasn't changed. Your perception of risk tracks what's memorable, not what's probable.
summary.university: Tversky & Kahneman (1973) demonstrated that probability and frequency judgements are driven by ease of mental retrieval rather than actual statistical frequency — a process relying on hippocampal-PFC retrieval circuits. Emotional vividness, recency, and media coverage all inflate availability, causing overestimation of dramatic risks (terrorism, plane crashes) and underestimation of mundane ones (heart disease). Within behavioural economics and public health, availability cascades (Kuran & Sunstein) describe how media amplification creates self-reinforcing cycles of perceived risk.
summary.research: Schwarz et al. (1991) revealed that retrieval fluency, not just content, drives judgements: asking for 12 reasons one is assertive (hard) led participants to rate themselves less assertive than asking for 6 (easy) — proving that difficulty of retrieval signals low frequency. Computationally, availability implements an importance-sampling approximation to Bayesian inference where the sampling distribution is biased toward memorable, easily generated instances. An open question is whether availability and representativeness doubly dissociate neurally: vmPFC lesion patients show impaired representativeness but not availability, suggesting separable substrates — but this dissociation has not been systematically replicated.

real_life_examples: Fearing flying after seeing a plane crash in the news
Parents over-restricting children after high-profile abduction cases
Overestimating crime rates after watching true crime content
COVID risk perception varying with local case visibility

title: Mirror Neurons & Empathy

summary.beginner: Some brain cells fire both when you do something and when you watch someone else do it — like your brain is secretly practising along. The best everyday analogy: wincing when someone else stubs their toe. Your motor and pain circuits partially activate just from witnessing their pain.
summary.university: The putative human mirror neuron system (MNS) spans the inferior frontal gyrus (IFG, BA44/45), inferior parietal lobule (IPL), and premotor cortex (BA6). Originally discovered by Rizzolatti et al. in macaque area F5 (1996), mirror neurons respond to both executed and observed goal-directed actions. Within social and cognitive neuroscience, the MNS is proposed to underlie action understanding, imitation, and affective empathy — though human evidence relies largely on indirect fMRI and EEG mu-suppression measures.
summary.research: The associative learning hypothesis (Heyes, 2010) argues that mirror neuron properties emerge from Hebbian co-activation of sensory and motor representations during action observation and execution — no specialised 'mirror system' is required. Computationally, this is a predictive coding account: the motor system forward-models observed actions and generates error signals when predictions fail, producing empathy as a residual mismatch signal. An open question — whether the 'broken mirror' hypothesis of autism holds — has not survived scrutiny; autistic individuals show comparable MNS activation in ecological settings, shifting debate toward social motivation deficits instead.

real_life_examples: Wincing when watching someone stub their toe
Yawning when others yawn
Babies imitating facial expressions within hours of birth
Feeling secondhand embarrassment for others

title: Framing Effect

summary.beginner: The framing effect is the finding that identical information presented in different frames produces different decisions. The analogy: the same glass of water is half full or half empty — not just as a metaphor, but as a measurable effect on what people choose to do about it. The concrete example: medical patients choose surgery at far higher rates when told '90% of patients survive' than when told '10% of patients die,' despite these being mathematically identical statements.
summary.university: Kahneman & Tversky's Asian Disease Problem (1981) is canonical: under gain framing ('200 will be saved'), people prefer the certain option; under loss framing ('400 will die'), they prefer the gamble — same outcomes, reversed preference. This is consistent with Prospect Theory's value function: concavity in gains (risk aversion) and convexity in losses (risk-seeking). Neural substrates involve vmPFC tracking expected value, while amygdala and insula provide frame-dependent affective salience that modulates choice. Within behavioural economics and health communication, framing affects medical decision-making, policy adoption, and consumer behaviour.
summary.research: De Martino et al. (2006) used fMRI to show that amygdala activation mediates framing susceptibility: individuals with higher amygdala responses to loss-framed choices show stronger framing effects, while vmPFC damage abolishes framing sensitivity. Regulatory focus theory (Higgins, 1997) predicts that promotion-focused individuals are more sensitive to gain frames; prevention-focused to loss frames — a personality moderator consistent with differential orbitofrontal-amygdala coupling. An open question is whether framing effects are cognitively penetrable — can people who know about the framing effect be trained to make frame-independent decisions? — evidence suggests that awareness does not eliminate the effect, supporting an automatic affective origin.

real_life_examples: '95% survival rate' vs. '5% mortality rate' — same data, different reactions
Ground beef '75% lean' vs. '25% fat'
Organ donation opt-in vs. opt-out default framing
Negotiation gains vs. losses framing in diplomacy

title: Neuroplasticity

summary.beginner: The brain is not fixed hardware — it rewires itself based on experience, like a muscle that grows with use. London taxi drivers who memorise thousands of routes develop measurably larger hippocampi compared to non-drivers — a direct demonstration that learning physically reshapes the brain.
summary.university: Neuroplasticity spans synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD at glutamatergic synapses), structural plasticity (dendritic spine remodelling, axonal sprouting), and adult neurogenesis in the dentate gyrus and olfactory bulb. Hebb's postulate ('neurons that fire together wire together') describes associative synaptic strengthening. Within developmental and systems neuroscience, critical periods of heightened cortical plasticity are opened by BDNF and closed by maturation of parvalbumin-positive GABAergic interneurons.
summary.research: BDNF/TrkB signalling is the master effector; Arc (activity-regulated cytoskeleton-associated protein) is an immediate-early gene required for AMPA receptor trafficking and LTP consolidation, first characterised by Guzowski et al. (1999). Mathematically, plasticity follows a BCM learning rule where the synaptic modification threshold θ slides as a function of postsynaptic activity history, enabling both potentiation and depression. The open question is the extent to which pharmacological reopening of critical-period plasticity in adults (via ketamine or SSRIs) is clinically exploitable for amblyopia, PTSD, and addiction recovery.

real_life_examples: London taxi drivers having enlarged hippocampi
Musicians having expanded motor cortex for finger movements
Stroke patients regaining function through rehabilitation
Meditation measurably increasing grey matter density

title: Intrinsic Motivation

summary.beginner: Intrinsic motivation is doing something for its own sake — because it's interesting, enjoyable, or meaningful — rather than for any external reward. The analogy: intrinsic motivation is internal combustion; extrinsic motivation is being pushed. The concrete example: children playing with a toy freely, then being paid for it — studies show they play less with the toy afterward, having been 'explained' by external reward rather than curiosity (the overjustification effect).
summary.university: Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985) grounds intrinsic motivation in three basic psychological needs: autonomy (perceived locus of causality is internal), competence (feeling effective), and relatedness. The overjustification effect occurs when extrinsic rewards shift perceived causality from internal to external, undermining subsequent intrinsic motivation. Neural substrates involve mPFC and striatum (autonomous choice encoding), with VTA→mPFC projections encoding intrinsic value distinct from VTA→NAc incentive salience. This falls within motivational psychology and educational neuroscience.
summary.research: Deci et al.'s (1999) meta-analysis of 128 studies confirmed that tangible, expected, contingent rewards reliably undermine intrinsic motivation, while unexpected rewards and positive feedback do not — supporting the cognitive evaluation theory (CET) sub-framework of SDT. fMRI contrasts of autonomous versus controlled motivation show medial PFC (self-referential) and posterior parietal (agency network) engagement for autonomous choices (Murayama et al., 2010). An open question is whether the distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation reflects discrete neural systems or a continuum of goal internalisation, as SDT's organismic integration theory predicts — and whether this continuum is reflected in a continuous gradient of vmPFC engagement.

real_life_examples: Losing track of time when absorbed in a creative project
Children stopping a hobby after getting paid for it
Autonomy at work predicting employee retention better than salary
Gamification backfiring by crowding out intrinsic interest

title: Sleep & Memory Consolidation

summary.beginner: Sleep is not passive rest — it's when the brain actively replays and transfers the day's memories into long-term storage. The analogy: sleep is the overnight backup that saves your brain's daily work to permanent storage. A concrete example: learning a new motor skill (like a piano sequence) and sleeping before being tested produces dramatically better performance than staying awake — sleep physically consolidates the motor programme.
summary.university: During NREM slow-wave sleep, hippocampal sharp-wave ripples (SWRs, ~80–100 Hz) replay experience sequences, triggering thalamic sleep spindles (12–15 Hz) and neocortical slow oscillations (0.75 Hz) in a precisely coordinated three-way coupling. This hippocampo-cortical dialogue transfers memory traces from hippocampus to distributed neocortical storage. REM sleep subsequently strengthens emotional memories and procedural abstractions. Sleep deprivation both prevents consolidation and impairs the next day's hippocampal encoding capacity. Within systems neuroscience, sleep consolidation is the canonical model of active memory processing.
summary.research: Targeted memory reactivation (TMR) — playing the sound associated with a learned memory during NREM sleep to trigger replay — causally strengthens that memory's subsequent recall (Rudoy et al., 2009), demonstrating that replay drives consolidation rather than merely correlating with it. Walker & Stickgold's (2006) review formalised the two-stage model: hippocampal encoding → NREM-dependent SWR replay → neocortical consolidation. An open question is the function of REM sleep in memory: whether it performs emotional memory consolidation specifically (Walker's model) or primarily engages in associative memory integration across distantly related episodes (Stickgold & Walker's opposing view) — the two frameworks make distinct predictions about which memories benefit most from REM.

real_life_examples: Sleeping on a problem and waking with a solution
All-night studying backfiring on exam performance
Napping after learning improving retention
PTSD nightmares as maladaptive fear memory consolidation

title: Sunk Cost Fallacy

summary.beginner: The sunk cost fallacy is continuing a failing course of action because of resources already invested — past investment that cannot be recovered. The analogy: you're in a hole and keep digging because you've already put so much effort into digging it. A concrete example: finishing a dreadful book because you've already read 300 pages — the rational move is to stop, since those 300 pages are gone regardless, but the brain treats past investment as a reason to continue.
summary.university: Rational decision theory prescribes ignoring sunk costs and basing choices only on future expected value. Arkes & Blumer (1985) demonstrated the fallacy experimentally: participants who paid more for ski tickets attended more despite injury — sunk cost inflating continuation value. vmPFC integrates past investment inappropriately into subjective value computation; ACC signals the switching cost as a form of loss; OFC counterfactual processing generates anticipated regret from 'giving up.' Within behavioural economics and organisational behaviour, this is studied as escalation of commitment and the Concorde Fallacy.
summary.research: Computational models frame sunk cost as a failure of Bayesian updating: prior investment serves as spurious evidence for future success — a non-stationary inference error. Individual differences in sunk cost susceptibility correlate with vmPFC volume and FC with ACC. Importantly, the effect is absent in vmPFC patients and attenuated in young children (under 5), suggesting it is a learned inference habit rather than an innate cognitive constraint — acquired via social norms of commitment and waste aversion. An open question is whether the primary driver is loss aversion (treating continuation as a gain versus stopping as a loss) or anticipated regret (OFC forward-simulation of the 'wasted investment' feeling) — these make different intervention predictions.

real_life_examples: Finishing an awful book because you've read half
Staying in a bad relationship due to years already invested
Governments continuing failed military campaigns
Entrepreneurs refusing to pivot despite market evidence

title: Anterior Cingulate Cortex

summary.beginner: The anterior cingulate cortex is your brain's error-detection alarm — it fires whenever something unexpected happens or when your actions conflict with your goals. Think of it as the little voice that says 'wait, that didn't go to plan' — like the jolt of awareness when you reach for your phone only to remember you deliberately left it in another room.
summary.university: The ACC divides into a dorsal cognitive division (dACC, BA32) and a rostral affective division (rACC, BA24/25). dACC detects response conflict and error via the error-related negativity (ERN) signal — a negative-going ERP at ~50ms post-error — and signals dlPFC to increase cognitive control. rACC regulates emotional responses via projections to the amygdala and autonomic centres. Within cognitive neuroscience, ACC is a core node of the salience network (alongside the anterior insula).
summary.research: Botvinick et al.'s conflict-monitoring theory (2001) formalises dACC function: it computes conflict as the dot product of simultaneously active incompatible response representations and passes a scalar control signal to dlPFC. Mathematically, control allocation follows a cost-benefit calculation where ACC conflict signal is weighted against estimated reward of resolving conflict. An open question is whether rACC hypoactivation in depression reflects a failure of emotion regulation per se, or a deficit in the reward-value coding of social stimuli that the rACC normally performs.

real_life_examples: Recognizing a mistake and correcting it
Staying calm in stressful situations
Managing pain through mindfulness

title: Insula

summary.beginner: The insula is your brain's body-translator — it converts raw physiological signals (heart rate, gut tension, pain, temperature) into conscious feelings. The analogy: it's the instrument panel that turns engine sensor readings into warning lights you can actually understand. A concrete example is the 'gut feeling' of unease before a risky decision — your insula is reading your body's stress signals and flagging them as emotion.
summary.university: The insular cortex divides into a posterior granular region (primary interoceptive cortex, integrating visceral and pain signals) and an anterior agranular region (higher-order emotional representation). Anterior insula is a core hub of the salience network with the dACC, and receives convergent input from interoception, taste, and limbic structures. Within affective neuroscience and computational psychiatry, insula is the substrate of subjective emotional feeling — Craig (2009) proposed it generates the 'global emotional moment' underlying consciousness.
summary.research: Critchley et al. (2004) established that interoceptive accuracy (heartbeat detection performance) correlates with right anterior insula grey matter and predicts anxiety and emotional intensity. Computationally, the insula is proposed to compute a predictive model of body state — interoceptive inference — where prediction errors between expected and actual visceral signals generate affective feelings (Seth & Friston, 2016). An open question is whether interoceptive training (heartbeat-tracking biofeedback, body-scan meditation) causally improves emotional regulation by strengthening insula-PFC circuits or merely improves introspective metacognition.

real_life_examples: Feeling your heartbeat during a stressful situation
Recognizing emotions in others
Making decisions based on gut feelings

title: Thalamus

summary.beginner: The thalamus is the brain's central switchboard — almost every sensory signal (sight, sound, touch, pain) routes through it before reaching the cortex. Think of it like an airport hub: all flights (sensory inputs) must pass through before reaching their destination terminals (sensory cortices). A concrete example: your ability to focus on a conversation while ignoring background noise depends on thalamic gating filtering out irrelevant signals.
summary.university: The thalamus comprises ~30 functionally distinct nuclei: sensory relay nuclei (lateral geniculate body for vision, medial geniculate for audition, ventral posterior for somatosensation), motor nuclei (ventral anterior/lateral, linked to basal ganglia and cerebellum), and associative nuclei (mediodorsal to PFC, pulvinar to parietal cortex). The thalamic reticular nucleus (TRN), a GABAergic shell, provides competitive surround inhibition that gates signal transmission. Within systems neuroscience, thalamocortical loops are critical for consciousness and selective attention.
summary.research: Thalamocortical oscillations — spindles (12–15 Hz) from TRN–relay nucleus loops and slow oscillations (0.75 Hz) from cortex — coordinate memory consolidation during NREM sleep; Steriade's work (2001) established their cellular basis. Computationally, the thalamus is modelled as a dynamic routing gate implementing selective attention via winner-take-all inhibition through TRN, formalised in predictive coding frameworks as a precision-weighting mechanism. An open question is the causal role of mediodorsal thalamus in working memory: whether its activity is upstream of PFC (driving PFC representations) or a gating relay (modulating access of information into PFC).

real_life_examples: Tuning out background noise to focus on one conversation
Pain signals reaching conscious awareness only via thalamic relay
Thalamic stroke causing sudden loss of sensation on one body side
Sleep spindles during NREM generated by thalamocortical loops

title: Basal Ganglia

summary.beginner: The basal ganglia are your brain's action-selector — they decide which behaviours get a 'go' signal and which get suppressed. The best analogy: they're a voting system where thousands of neural circuits compete, and the basal ganglia declare the winner. A concrete example is driving a familiar route on autopilot while your conscious mind thinks about something else — the basal ganglia are running the stored motor programme without prefrontal involvement.
summary.university: The basal ganglia comprise the striatum (caudate nucleus, putamen, nucleus accumbens), globus pallidus external (GPe) and internal (GPi), subthalamic nucleus (STN), and substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) and reticulata (SNr). The direct pathway (D1-MSNs → GPi → thalamus) facilitates selected actions; the indirect pathway (D2-MSNs → GPe → STN → GPi) suppresses competing actions. Dopamine from SNc biases selection via opponent D1/D2 receptor mechanisms. Within motor neuroscience and computational psychiatry, basal ganglia dysfunction underlies Parkinson's (dopamine loss), Huntington's (D2-MSN degeneration), and OCD (hyperactive cortico-striatal loops).
summary.research: Actor-critic reinforcement learning maps onto basal ganglia anatomy: the striatum implements the actor (action selection proportional to value), while dopamine RPE signals function as the critic (value estimation update), formalised by Joel & Weiner (1994) and extended by Dayan & Balleine (2002). Optogenetic studies (Kravitz et al., 2012) confirmed that D1-MSN stimulation promotes movement and D2-MSN stimulation suppresses it, providing causal proof of the direct/indirect pathway model. An open question is the role of thalamostriatal (versus corticostriatal) inputs in habit formation — thalamic projections from centromedian nucleus appear specifically important for initiating well-learned action sequences.

real_life_examples: Driving a familiar route on autopilot
Compulsive checking of phone driven by striatal habit loops
Parkinson's patients struggling to initiate movement
The 'frozen' feeling when trying to break a deeply ingrained habit

title: Corpus Callosum

summary.beginner: The corpus callosum is the brain's inter-hemisphere motorway — ~200 million nerve fibres connecting left and right brain so they can share information. The clearest demonstration of its importance: patients whose corpus callosum is surgically cut (for epilepsy) cannot name objects shown only to their left visual field, because the visual information reaches the right hemisphere but cannot reach the left hemisphere's language areas.
summary.university: The corpus callosum (CC) spans genu (connecting frontal lobes), body (sensorimotor), isthmus (posterior parietal and temporal), and splenium (occipital/temporal). It enables interhemispheric transfer of perceptual, motor, and cognitive information. Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) fractional anisotropy (FA) of CC white matter predicts processing speed, verbal IQ, and reading ability. Within neuropsychology and cognitive neuroscience, CC integrity is a sensitive biomarker of axonal health in ageing, multiple sclerosis, and traumatic brain injury.
summary.research: Sperry and Gazzaniga's split-brain studies (1960s–1970s) revealed functional hemispheric lateralisation — left hemisphere for language/sequential processing, right for visuospatial/holistic processing — by revealing what each hemisphere could and could not do in isolation. Mathematically, interhemispheric transfer time (IHTT, typically 3–5ms) is measurable via ERP latency asymmetry and predicts callosal conduction velocity. An open question is the functional significance of callosal inhibition versus excitation: the corpus callosum both transfers and suppresses activity between hemispheres, and it remains unclear which mechanism dominates for which cognitive functions.

real_life_examples: Playing piano requiring both hands to coordinate complex independent patterns
Split-brain patients unable to name objects shown to left visual field
Multiple sclerosis causing coordination problems via CC demyelination
Children's reading ability correlating with CC splenium integrity

title: Ventral Tegmental Area

summary.beginner: The VTA is the brain's dopamine fountain — a tiny cluster of neurons in the midbrain that sprays dopamine across the brain whenever something rewarding happens or is anticipated. The analogy: it's the motivational engine behind all desire and wanting. A concrete example: the surge of excitement you feel when you're about to open a gift comes directly from VTA dopamine neurons firing in anticipation.
summary.university: VTA dopaminergic neurons (A10 cell group) project via three pathways: mesolimbic (VTA → nucleus accumbens, amygdala, hippocampus — reward and motivation), mesocortical (VTA → PFC — executive function and working memory), and nigrostriatal (substantia nigra → dorsal striatum — motor control and habit learning). VTA receives glutamatergic drive from PFC and LH, GABAergic inhibition from NAc and VP, and cholinergic modulation from pedunculopontine tegmentum. Within systems neuroscience and addiction neurobiology, VTA is the origin of the mesolimbic dopamine system underlying reward learning.
summary.research: Schultz et al.'s landmark single-unit recordings in primates (1997) demonstrated that VTA dopamine neurons encode temporal difference reward prediction errors — firing at reward delivery initially, then shifting to conditioned stimulus onset with learning, and pausing at expected-reward omission. Computationally, this implements δ(t) = r(t) − V(t) in the Rescorla-Wagner / TD(λ) framework. An open question is the functional heterogeneity within VTA: a subset of VTA dopamine neurons respond to aversive events with burst firing (rather than pausing), suggesting motivational salience rather than pure reward valence coding — the precise circuit logic of this subset remains debated.

real_life_examples: The pleasurable anticipation before eating a favourite meal
Why the first hit of a drug feels most intense (sensitisation develops from VTA)
Romantic love activating VTA-to-NAc circuits in fMRI studies
Variable ratio schedules keeping gamblers engaged via VTA RPE signals

title: Locus Coeruleus

summary.beginner: The locus coeruleus is your brain's alertness broadcaster — it floods the entire cortex with noradrenaline the moment something surprising or threatening happens. The analogy: it's like a fire alarm for the brain, instantly switching all systems from 'idle' to 'high alert.' The concrete example: the sharp jolt of focus when your name is called unexpectedly across a noisy room.
summary.university: The LC contains ~15,000 noradrenergic neurons (per hemisphere) in the dorsal pons and is the sole source of norepinephrine (NE) to the cortex, hippocampus, thalamus, and cerebellum. LC neurons fire in two modes: tonic (low-rate background discharge, maintaining baseline vigilance) and phasic (high-frequency bursts to salient stimuli, driving attentional orienting). NE acts at α2A receptors on PFC spines to sharpen signal-to-noise and at α1/β receptors elsewhere to broaden arousal. Within cognitive neuroscience, LC-NE is a core substrate of arousal, attention, and stress-induced performance changes.
summary.research: Aston-Jones & Cohen's Adaptive Gain Theory (2005) formalises LC function: phasic LC activity optimises task-relevant signal detection (high gain), while tonic LC activity promotes disengagement and behavioural flexibility (exploration mode). Mathematically, this maps to a signal-detection framework where LC-NE scales d′ (sensitivity) for task-relevant stimuli. An open question is the LC's role in Alzheimer's disease: LC neurodegeneration precedes hippocampal atrophy by years (Braak et al.), and whether LC degeneration is a driver of early memory failure — rather than a consequence of hippocampal pathology — is a major current debate.

real_life_examples: The jolt of attention when your name is called in a crowd
Anxiety narrowing focus to the feared object only
Noradrenaline-based ADHD medications (atomoxetine) targeting LC projections
LC firing during REM sleep linked to vivid emotional dream content

title: Orbitofrontal Cortex

summary.beginner: The orbitofrontal cortex is the brain's value calculator — it assigns worth to options and updates those values when outcomes change. A useful analogy: it's like a stock ticker for your choices, constantly repricing options as new information arrives. The concrete example: the OFC is what lets you update your enjoyment of a restaurant when the quality declines — something Phineas Gage famously lost, making him reckless and unable to make socially adaptive decisions after OFC damage.
summary.university: The OFC (BA11/13/14) receives convergent sensory input (all modalities), plus amygdala, hippocampus, and striatal projections, and encodes subjective expected value on a common currency scale. Medial OFC encodes reward value; lateral OFC encodes punishment and signals the need for behavioural change via projections to dorsal striatum. OFC is critical for reversal learning — updating behaviour when reward contingencies reverse — and for extinction of conditioned fear. Within behavioural economics and affective neuroscience, OFC is the substrate of flexible value-based decision-making.
summary.research: O'Doherty et al. (2001) demonstrated human OFC responds to primary (taste) and secondary (money) reward on a common value scale using fMRI. Single-unit OFC recordings in primates show dynamic repricing within a single trial, implementing a fast form of credit assignment distinct from slower hippocampal consolidation. Computationally, OFC may implement a model-based Reinforcement Learning 'world model' that permits flexible goal pursuit; an open question is whether OFC stores this model or dynamically retrieves it via orbitofrontal-hippocampal loops, with implications for understanding compulsive behaviour disorders where OFC-striatal loops become pathologically fixed.

real_life_examples: Updating preferences when a favourite restaurant declines in quality
OFC damage in Phineas Gage causing reckless decision-making
Difficulty adapting to rule changes (reversal learning impairment) in OFC lesion patients
OFC activity predicting how much people will pay for a product in neuroeconomics studies

title: Dunning-Kruger Effect

summary.beginner: The Dunning-Kruger effect is the paradox that the less someone knows about a domain, the more confident they tend to be — because they lack the knowledge to even know what they don't know. The everyday analogy: a first-year medical student who has learned the names of a few conditions feels briefly like they could diagnose anything; a consultant physician is acutely aware of diagnostic uncertainty. The example: novice drivers rating themselves as 'above average' in skill immediately after passing their test.
summary.university: Kruger & Dunning (1999) proposed that metacognitive deficits — specifically, the inability to accurately assess one's own performance — explain overconfidence in low performers across domains (logical reasoning, grammar, humour). The effect has two components: poor performers overestimate absolute performance, and experts underestimate relative performance (because they assume their domain is easy for everyone). Within cognitive psychology and educational science, this is studied as calibration, metacognitive accuracy, and overconfidence bias.
summary.research: The effect's robustness has been challenged: Gignac & Zajenkowski (2020) and Nuhfer et al. (2016) argue the original pattern largely reflects statistical artefact — Better-Than-Average effect plus regression to the mean — rather than a uniquely metacognitive phenomenon. Computationally, miscalibration can be modelled as a noisy signal-detection process where lower competence narrows the discriminability (d′) between good and poor performance, making both discrimination and self-assessment less reliable. An open question is whether metacognitive accuracy is a domain-general trait (reflecting frontal metacognitive systems, indexed by anterior PFC grey matter) or a domain-specific skill acquired with expertise — evidence currently supports both accounts in different paradigms.

real_life_examples: New drivers rating themselves as above average after passing their test
First-year students feeling more confident than final-year students after initial exposure
Novice investors dismissing professional fund managers
Armchair experts confidently correcting specialists on social media

title: Representativeness Heuristic

summary.beginner: The representativeness heuristic is the tendency to judge probability by resemblance to a mental prototype, ignoring actual statistical likelihood. The analogy: if someone looks like our mental image of a librarian — quiet, neat, precise — we assume they are a librarian, even though there are far more truck drivers than librarians. The classic example is the Linda Problem: told Linda is a passionate feminist bank teller, people rate 'feminist bank teller' as more probable than simply 'bank teller,' violating basic probability.
summary.university: Tversky & Kahneman (1974) identified representativeness as a primary heuristic underlying probabilistic judgements. It drives two key errors: base rate neglect (ignoring prior probabilities in favour of similarity-to-prototype) and the conjunction fallacy (rating specific scenarios as more probable than general ones when the specific scenario better matches the prototype). Striatal circuits encode category membership probability; vmPFC weights prior probability (base rate) — and representativeness-driven errors arise when prototype similarity overrides vmPFC base-rate input. This falls within judgment and decision-making and cognitive psychology.
summary.research: Griffiths & Tenenbaum's (2006) rational-process Bayesian analysis argues representativeness is not purely a bias but a reasonable first approximation to likelihood estimation from sparse data — base rate neglect emerges when the prior is flat or unknown. Computationally, representativeness maps to a likelihood ratio: P(evidence|hypothesis) evaluated via feature similarity, without normalisation by P(evidence). An open question is whether representativeness and availability doubly dissociate in lesion studies — vmPFC damage selectively impairs base-rate use (a representativeness failure) while hippocampal lesions impair availability — suggesting distinct neural implementations of these two heuristics.

real_life_examples: Assuming a bearded man in a lab coat is a scientist, not an actor
Doctors over-diagnosing rare diseases when symptoms match perfectly
Investors assuming past winners will continue winning
Profiling based on stereotypical appearance rather than statistical likelihood

title: Bystander Effect

summary.beginner: The bystander effect is the phenomenon where the more people witness an emergency, the less likely any single person is to help — because everyone assumes someone else will act. The analogy: responsibility is diluted like paint in water — the more witnesses, the thinner each person's sense of obligation. A concrete example: calling for help in a crowd by addressing 'someone' often fails; saying 'you in the blue jacket, call 999' succeeds by eliminating diffusion of responsibility.
summary.university: Darley & Latané (1968) identified two mechanisms: diffusion of responsibility (each bystander feels their obligation diluted by the number of others present) and pluralistic ignorance (each person looks to others' calm reaction as a signal that intervention is unnecessary, producing collective inaction). Medial PFC circuits mediating moral agency and personal responsibility are suppressed by group context in neuroimaging paradigms. Within social psychology, bystander research is foundational to emergency intervention theory and organisational responsibility.
summary.research: Fischer et al.'s meta-analysis (2011) revealed important boundary conditions: in dangerous situations, larger groups increase helping — social facilitation of intervention overrides diffusion of responsibility when threat is unambiguous. Computationally, the bystander effect models as a game-theoretic volunteer's dilemma where each bystander waits to see if others will intervene (defecting), and the probability of intervention decreases as a function of group size following 1-(1-p)^n logic. An open question is whether digital bystander effects — non-intervention in online harassment — follow the same diffusion-of-responsibility logic or are driven by anonymity and reduced social presence cues, which have distinct neural substrates.

real_life_examples: Kitty Genovese attack witnessed by 38 reported bystanders without intervention
People walking past a collapsed person when surrounded by a crowd
Cyberbullying ignored because 'someone else will report it'
Direct address ('You, in the red jacket — call 999!') overriding the bystander effect

title: Just-World Hypothesis

summary.beginner: The just-world hypothesis is the deep-seated belief that people get what they deserve and deserve what they get — a belief that feels psychologically reassuring because it implies you can control your own fate by being good. The everyday analogy: karma. A concrete and troubling example: people spontaneously blame rape victims for their own assaults, attributing their suffering to their own behaviour rather than accepting that random injustice is possible.
summary.university: Lerner (1965, 1980) proposed that belief in a just world is a fundamental cognitive bias serving a defensive function: perceiving the world as just protects the perceiver from confronting the terrifying reality of random suffering. When faced with unjust suffering, the cognitive paths are: (1) accept the injustice and feel threatened, or (2) derogate the victim (they deserved it). The temporal parietal junction (TPJ) and medial PFC, involved in mental state attribution and causal inference, generate the attributional narratives that blame victims. Within social psychology, just-world belief is studied as a system-justifying ideology that predicts tolerance of inequality.
summary.research: Jost et al.'s system justification theory (2004) extended Lerner's framework, showing that disadvantaged groups also endorse just-world beliefs — a form of motivated cognition that preserves psychological stability at the cost of challenging unjust systems. Computationally, victim derogation can be modelled as Bayesian attribution where the prior (world is just) heavily constrains causal inference, forcing observers to construct victim-blaming narratives to preserve the prior. An open question is whether just-world beliefs operate through propositional reasoning (accessible to deliberative challenge) or through implicit automatic attributional processes — evidence from implicit association tests suggests the latter.

real_life_examples: Assuming rape victims 'asked for it' by their behaviour or dress
Believing poor people are lazy rather than structurally disadvantaged
Spiritual narratives of karma justifying suffering
Blaming ill people for their lifestyle choices rather than acknowledging chance

title: Survivorship Bias

summary.beginner: Survivorship bias is seeing only the winners because the losers have disappeared from view — and then drawing conclusions about 'what works' from this unrepresentative sample. The analogy: studying only the ships that returned from battle to decide where to add armour. The concrete example: the reason entrepreneurship advice from successful billionaires is systematically misleading — the millions who followed identical strategies and failed are invisible.
summary.university: Survivorship bias is a form of selection bias where observed data is systematically unrepresentative because failed cases are removed (they 'didn't survive' to be observed). Wald's WWII aircraft analysis is canonical: the correct insight was to reinforce where returning planes were undamaged — that's where shot-down planes were hit. In financial economics, survivorship bias inflates historical mutual fund performance by 1–2% annually when failed funds are excluded from databases. Within research methodology and cognitive psychology, this is a pervasive threat to external validity.
summary.research: Brown et al. (1992) quantified survivorship bias in mutual fund performance databases, showing that including defunct funds substantially reduces average reported returns. Computationally, survivorship bias creates a conditional distribution P(observed | survived) that diverges from the marginal P(observed), and the magnitude of bias equals the covariance between outcome variable and survival probability. An open question is whether counterfactual simulation — imagining the missing failures — is a reliable debiasing strategy, or whether the cognitive load required to generate realistic failure scenarios renders it practically ineffective under time pressure and motivated cognition.

real_life_examples: Dropouts like Jobs and Zuckerberg making college seem unnecessary (forgetting millions of dropout failures)
World War II planes reinforced in the wrong places without Wald's insight
Only profitable hedge funds appearing in performance rankings
Advice from successful people being systematically biased toward their survivorship path

title: Planning Fallacy

summary.beginner: The planning fallacy is the universal tendency to underestimate how long, expensive, and complicated a project will be, while overestimating how smooth progress will be. The analogy: everyone's a better driver in their head than reality; everyone's a more efficient worker on paper than in practice. The concrete example: the Sydney Opera House was budgeted at $7 million and 4 years; it cost $102 million and took 14 years — a textbook case.
summary.university: Kahneman & Tversky (1979) identified the inside view as the core driver: planners focus on the specific scenario's optimistic narrative rather than the base rate of similar past projects (outside view). This involves default mode network nodes — mPFC and hippocampus — which construct plausible future narratives biased toward positive outcomes. Unpacking fallacy compounds the effect: detailed task decomposition inflates estimates for sub-tasks while leaving total-project estimates anchored to the original optimistic plan. This is studied within behavioural economics, project management, and forecasting research.
summary.research: Flyvbjerg et al. (2002) analysed 258 transportation infrastructure projects across 20 countries, finding cost overruns in 86% of cases, with average overruns of 45% — robust across decades and geography. Reference class forecasting (Flyvbjerg's debiasing procedure) anchors predictions to the historical distribution of comparable past projects, achieving substantially better calibration than inside-view estimation. Computationally, the planning fallacy reflects optimistic probability weighting: future effort is discounted hyperbolically, and the distribution over outcomes is over-concentrated on best-case scenarios. An open question is whether the fallacy is reducible by simple procedural interventions (outside-view prompting) alone, or whether motivated optimism must also be addressed through accountability structures.

real_life_examples: The Sydney Opera House: budgeted at $7M, cost $102M and took 14 years instead of 4
Software projects routinely taking 2-3× estimated time
Students beginning essays the night before despite knowing from past experience
Home renovation projects doubling in cost and duration

title: Illusory Correlation

summary.beginner: Illusory correlation is perceiving a relationship between two things when no actual relationship exists — usually because both things are distinctive or memorable. The analogy: your brain is a pattern-detection machine so eager to find patterns that it invents them. A concrete example: believing that you always get stuck in traffic when you're running late — when in reality you're just more likely to notice the traffic on those days.
summary.university: Hamilton & Gifford (1976) demonstrated illusory correlation in social perception: rare behaviours committed by minority-group members are over-associated because both the group and the behaviour are distinctive, increasing their joint memorability. This is a distinctiveness-based mechanism relying on hippocampal-dependent associative binding. A separate expectancy-based mechanism reinforces pre-existing stereotypes. Within social psychology and cognitive psychology, illusory correlation is a key mechanism in stereotype formation and maintenance.
summary.research: Working memory load selectively disrupts distinctiveness-based illusory correlation (Stroessner et al., 1992) — implicating controlled attentional processes in its generation, unlike expectancy-based illusory correlation which persists under load. Computationally, the bias can be modelled as a χ² association statistic applied to a biased memory sample: the conditional probability P(negative behaviour | minority group) is overestimated because memory samples minority-behaviour co-occurrences at higher rates than they actually occur. An open question is whether illusory correlation persists in large data environments (where the law of large numbers should correct it) or whether algorithmic recommendation systems amplify it by preferentially surfacing memorable extreme co-occurrences.

real_life_examples: Sports fans believing specific rituals cause their team to win
Stereotype that minority groups commit more crimes based on rare vivid incidents
Parents believing vaccines caused their child's autism from temporal coincidence
Financial traders believing market patterns that are statistical noise

title: Gambler's Fallacy

summary.beginner: The gambler's fallacy is the mistaken belief that after a streak of one outcome in a random sequence, the opposite outcome is 'due.' The analogy: flipping a coin has no memory — each flip is independent — but the brain insists the coin 'owes' you a tail after five heads. A concrete example: roulette players who bet heavily on black after a long red streak, convinced the wheel must 'balance out.'
summary.university: The gambler's fallacy reflects a violation of probabilistic independence: people believe short sequences of random events should resemble the long-run distribution — what Tversky & Kahneman called 'local representativeness.' This produces negative recency bias (opposite outcome is expected) in contrast to the hot-hand fallacy (positive recency for non-random processes). ACC tracks sequential outcome prediction errors; its signals drive irrational expectation updating. Within behavioural economics and cognitive psychology, the fallacy appears in financial trading, medical diagnosis, legal sentencing, and sports.
summary.research: Chen et al. (2016) documented the gambler's fallacy in real-world high-stakes decisions: asylum judges were less likely to grant asylum after consecutive approvals, and baseball umpires called fewer strikes after calling consecutive strikes — naturalistic evidence outside laboratory settings. Computationally, the fallacy emerges in Bayesian learners with a prior that the data-generating process is a balanced urn (with replacement confusion) — formally equivalent to the 'sampling without replacement' model producing anti-correlation. An open question is whether the gambler's fallacy and hot-hand fallacy are mutually exclusive (you believe one or the other) or whether they co-exist in the same individuals, applied to different perceived process types — evidence suggests people distinguish between random and skill-based processes, applying gambler's fallacy to the former and hot-hand to the latter.

real_life_examples: Roulette players betting on black after five consecutive reds
Basketball fans believing a player is 'due' to hit a shot after misses
Lottery players choosing numbers that haven't appeared recently
Asylum judges granting asylum less often after several consecutive approvals

title: Spotlight Effect

summary.beginner: The spotlight effect is the overestimation of how much other people notice and pay attention to you. The analogy: you feel like you're on stage with a spotlight on every mistake — but actually, the audience is busy thinking about their own spotlights. The concrete example: spilling coffee on your shirt and feeling certain everyone notices it all day — while in reality most people have already forgotten within seconds.
summary.university: Gilovich, Medvec & Savitsky (2000) demonstrated experimentally: participants wearing an embarrassing T-shirt estimated ~50% of observers noticed; actual observers noticed ~25%. The effect arises from anchoring on one's own salient self-perception and insufficiently adjusting downward for others' limited attention. Theory of mind circuits — mPFC and TPJ — use self-experience as a starting point to model others' mental states, producing egocentric bias in the adjustment. Within social cognition, this is studied alongside the curse of knowledge and audience design failures.
summary.research: The spotlight effect and related self-consciousness biases are amplified by social anxiety, which engages heightened self-monitoring circuits (mPFC and dorsal ACC) and produces overestimation of observer attention proportional to anxiety severity. Computationally, the error can be modelled as an anchoring-and-adjustment process where the anchor is one's own perspective (strong prior) and adjustment toward others' perspective is systematically insufficient — matching the anchoring literature's computational account. An open question is whether decentring interventions — deliberate perspective-taking away from the self — can reliably reduce spotlight effect magnitude, or whether self-focused attention is so automatic that explicit instruction cannot override it.

real_life_examples: Agonising over a small stain only you have noticed on your shirt
Assuming everyone heard your stomach growl in a quiet room
Overthinking a verbal slip that others immediately forgot
Reluctance to exercise in public due to perceived scrutiny

title: Emotional Regulation

summary.beginner: Emotional regulation is the set of strategies your brain uses to manage how intensely you feel and express emotions. The analogy: it's the thermostat for your emotional temperature, letting you turn down the heat of anger or turn up warmth toward someone you're frustrated with. A concrete everyday example: consciously reframing a job rejection as 'useful feedback' rather than 'proof I'm not good enough' — this shifts the emotional meaning and reduces distress.
summary.university: Gross's process model (1998, 2015) identifies five regulation strategies: situation selection, situation modification, attentional deployment, cognitive reappraisal, and response modulation. Cognitive reappraisal — reinterpreting the emotional meaning of an event — is most adaptive, engaging vlPFC (BA47) and dlPFC to down-regulate amygdala activity via direct inhibitory projections. Suppression (modulating expressive response) reduces observable emotion but not subjective experience, and has social costs. Within affective science and clinical psychology, reappraisal capacity predicts psychological resilience and mental health outcomes.
summary.research: Ochsner & Gross (2005) established the neural model of reappraisal using fMRI: vlPFC/dlPFC activation negatively correlates with amygdala activation during reappraisal, providing the neural substrate for top-down emotion regulation. Computationally, reappraisal updates the generative model of a situation — changing the appraisal variable (meaning) that drives predicted affect, rather than changing the affective response directly. An open question is whether habitual regulation strategy selection is itself regulated by prefrontal executive resources (making regulation failure under stress a capacity depletion problem) or by automatic affective appraisal processes that precede deliberate regulation — the timing and sequencing of regulation initiation are actively debated.

real_life_examples: Taking deep breaths before a difficult conversation
Reframing a job rejection as useful feedback rather than failure
Counting to ten before responding when angry
Therapy teaching clients to observe rather than fuse with their emotions

title: Alexithymia

summary.beginner: Alexithymia literally means 'no words for feelings' — it's the difficulty of identifying and labelling your own emotional states. The analogy: imagine your body is sending signals in a language you can't read — you feel something is physically wrong (tense, nauseated, restless) but can't decode what emotion that is. A concrete example: someone who, after a distressing encounter, reports only 'my stomach hurts' while their partner can see they're clearly upset.
summary.university: Alexithymia is a multidimensional trait measured by the Toronto Alexithymia Scale (TAS-20), comprising difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings, and externally oriented thinking. It is not a discrete disorder but a continuously distributed trait (~10% of the population scoring highly). High alexithymia is associated with reduced right anterior insula and ACC activation in response to emotional imagery, reflecting impaired interoceptive-evaluative signal integration. Within clinical psychology and affective neuroscience, alexithymia predicts elevated rates of somatic disorders, substance misuse, and eating disorders.
summary.research: Bird et al.
