(2010) demonstrated that alexithymia — not autism per se — accounts for reduced empathic accuracy in autistic individuals, by showing alexithymia scores mediate the autism-empathy relationship when measured independently. Computationally, alexithymia may reflect a failure of interoceptive inference: weak prior precision weighting of body-state signals reduces their influence on emotional appraisal, producing emotional 'deafness' to one's own physiology (Seth, 2013). An open question is whether alexithymia is an acquired deficit (from early emotional environment) or a constitutional variation in insula development, and whether interoceptive training (heartbeat tracking, body-scan mindfulness) can causally reduce alexithymia severity.

real_life_examples: Knowing something is wrong — feeling tense and sick — but unable to name the emotion
Doctors' patients with alexithymia reporting only physical symptoms for what is actually anxiety
Difficulty understanding why one feels disconnected in social situations
Alexithymic individuals showing reduced emotional response to films while reporting physical discomfort

title: Affect Labeling

summary.beginner: Affect labelling — putting emotions into words — is one of the simplest and most powerful forms of emotion regulation. The analogy: naming a storm doesn't stop the storm, but it helps you decide what to do about it. The concrete example: writing in a journal about anxiety before a presentation actually reduces physiological arousal, not just subjective distress — a measurable calming effect from vocabulary alone.
summary.university: Lieberman et al. (2007) demonstrated that labelling emotional facial expressions ('afraid') activated right vlPFC (BA47) and simultaneously reduced amygdala activation relative to gender labelling ('female') — a direct PFC→amygdala inhibitory pathway operating automatically, without deliberate reappraisal effort. The mechanism is distinct from cognitive reappraisal: affect labelling does not require changing the emotional meaning, only encoding the emotional state in symbolic form. Within affective neuroscience and clinical psychology, affect labelling underpins mindfulness-based emotion regulation, written emotional disclosure (Pennebaker paradigm), and dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT).
summary.research: Torre & Lieberman's meta-analysis (2018) confirmed the robustness of affect labelling effects on amygdala and self-reported distress across paradigms. The disruption hypothesis (Arch & Craske) proposes labelling works by interrupting automatic emotional processing rather than resolving its meaning — a mechanistic distinction from reappraisal with implications for when each strategy is most effective. An open question is whether richer emotional vocabulary (emotion granularity) produces larger regulation benefits via more specific labelling precision — Barrett et al.'s work shows high-granularity individuals show smaller affective spill-over across contexts — but whether vocabulary training causally improves regulation or merely reflects underlying regulatory capacity is unresolved.

real_life_examples: Writing in a journal reducing anxiety about an upcoming event
Therapists asking 'what are you feeling right now?' to de-escalate distress
Children learning emotion words showing better self-regulation in school
Naming fears before a performance reducing pre-competition anxiety

title: Somatic Marker Hypothesis

summary.beginner: The somatic marker hypothesis says your body votes on your decisions — gut feelings, physical discomfort, or subtle tension serve as a fast emotional veto system that guides choices before conscious reasoning finishes computing. The analogy: imagine having a financial advisor who gives you a sick feeling in your stomach about a bad investment. Damasio found that patients with damage to the relevant brain area made catastrophically bad decisions despite intact intelligence — they had lost their body's advisory system.
summary.university: Damasio (1994) proposed that somatic markers — body-state representations associated with past emotional outcomes — bias ongoing decision-making via insula and vmPFC. The Iowa Gambling Task (IGT) operationalises this: healthy participants develop anticipatory skin conductance responses (SCRs) before choosing the bad decks and shift to advantageous choices, while vmPFC/OFC-lesioned patients show no anticipatory SCRs and continue making disadvantageous choices despite intact factual knowledge. Within affective neuroscience and clinical neuropsychology, this is studied as embodied decision-making and somatic cognition.
summary.research: Bechara et al.'s series of IGT studies (1994–2000) established that vmPFC/OFC patients fail to acquire anticipatory SCRs and make poor decisions, dissociating emotional decision-guidance from general intelligence. Computationally, somatic markers provide an as-if body loop: the OFC represents somatic states symbolically — simulating body-state outcomes without actual physiological change — enabling rapid value tagging. An open question is whether the somatic marker effect is specific to ambiguous decision contexts (where explicit outcome information is unavailable) or whether emotional body signals contribute even when full explicit information is provided — evidence is mixed, suggesting context-dependent somatic marker engagement.

effects: Poor decisions when markers are absent
real_life_examples: Gut feeling warning you away from a business deal that 'feels wrong'
vmPFC damage patients making consistently bad financial decisions despite knowing the rules
Anxious stomach before an important presentation serving as a preparatory signal
Experienced negotiators reading the room from embodied discomfort cues

title: Emotional Contagion

summary.beginner: Emotional contagion is the automatic transmission of emotions from one person to another through facial mimicry, vocal tone, and posture — you catch others' feelings without choosing to. The analogy: emotions spread like colds in a crowded room. The concrete example: laughing at a joke you didn't fully hear simply because everyone around you is laughing — your brain has automatically synchronised with the group's emotional state.
summary.university: Hatfield, Cacioppo & Rapson (1993) proposed that emotional contagion operates via two routes: primitive afferent feedback (unconscious facial/postural mimicry → proprioceptive feedback → shared emotion) and cognitive simulation (conscious perspective-taking). The putative mirror neuron system (IFG, premotor cortex) and limbic-insula circuits support mimicry-based contagion; hyperscanning fMRI demonstrates amygdala-insula coupling between emotional senders and receivers during face-to-face interaction. Within social psychology and organisational behaviour, emotional contagion propagates team mood states, leadership climate, and collective performance.
summary.research: Kramer et al.'s (2014) Facebook experiment — exposing 689,003 users to emotionally valenced newsfeeds and measuring their own post valence — provided causal evidence of emotional contagion in social networks at scale, though it sparked significant ethical debate. Computationally, emotional contagion in networks follows a threshold model where individuals adopt an emotional state when sufficient neighbours display it, producing tipping-point dynamics. An open question is whether emotional contagion via digital media (text, emoji) is mechanistically equivalent to face-to-face contagion (relying on the same motor-mimicry system) or operates through distinct cognitive routes requiring explicit affective interpretation.

real_life_examples: Laughing because everyone else is laughing, even without knowing why
Feeling anxious after spending time with a highly stressed colleague
Concert audiences reaching collective euphoria states
Social media mood contagion spreading across millions of users

title: Broaden-and-Build Theory

summary.beginner: The broaden-and-build theory says positive emotions do more than feel good — they expand your thinking and attention in the moment, and over time build lasting psychological resources: social bonds, creative skills, resilience, and physical health. The analogy: while negative emotions are a flashlight (narrow and urgent), positive emotions are floodlights — they illuminate more of the environment. A concrete example: children's free play is positive-emotion driven, and builds physical, cognitive, and social competence simultaneously.
summary.university: Fredrickson (1998, 2001) proposed that positive emotions evolved to broaden momentary thought-action repertoires (expanding attention, associative thinking, and behavioural flexibility) and to build lasting personal resources — functions distinct from negative emotions' narrow mobilisation for immediate threat. The undoing effect of positive emotions accelerates cardiovascular recovery from stress. Neural mechanisms involve mPFC and default mode network expansion of associative processing, and striatal dopamine modulating cognitive flexibility. Within positive psychology and affective neuroscience, broaden-and-build is the foundational model.
summary.research: Fredrickson et al. (2008) demonstrated that a 7-week loving-kindness meditation intervention causally increased positive affect, which in turn built psychological resources (mindfulness, purpose, social support) and reduced depressive symptoms — an upward spiral. Computationally, positive affect is associated with higher prior precision in predictive coding frameworks, expanding the scope of prediction (broader search through hypothesis space) analogous to a higher 'temperature' parameter in a softmax sampling distribution. An open question is whether positive affect's broadening effects are mediated specifically by dopaminergic neural flexibility (ventral striatum) or by top-down DMN facilitation of associative thought — these are dissociable predictions with distinct intervention implications.

real_life_examples: Children's play building physical, social and emotional skills simultaneously
Gratitude practices measurably improving wellbeing over 8 weeks
Creative breakthroughs following periods of relaxed positive mood
Happier employees generating more innovative solutions

title: Self-Determination Theory

summary.beginner: Self-Determination Theory says humans thrive when three core needs are met: autonomy (you choose), competence (you're capable), and relatedness (you belong). The analogy: think of these as the oxygen, water, and food of psychological life — meet all three and people flourish; deprive any one and motivation wilts. The concrete example: employees with flexible work arrangements and meaningful feedback consistently outperform those in micromanaged, rigid environments, even when pay is identical.
summary.university: Deci & Ryan's (1985, 2000) SDT proposes a motivational continuum from amotivation through external regulation, introjection, identification, integration, to intrinsic motivation — representing increasing internalisation of extrinsic goals. Basic psychological need satisfaction — particularly autonomy — predicts vitality, wellbeing, and intrinsic motivation across 77 cultures (Tay & Diener, 2011). Neural correlates of autonomy show mPFC and posterior parietal cortex (agency network) engagement for self-chosen versus assigned tasks. Within motivational psychology, organisational behaviour, and health psychology, SDT is the most empirically validated theory of human motivation.
summary.research: Williams et al.'s (2006) healthcare studies demonstrate that need-supportive physician communication predicts patient autonomous motivation for treatment adherence better than compliance-based approaches — operationalising SDT in clinical settings. Computationally, the SDT continuum can be modelled as a gradient of goal internalisation where autonomy-satisfying environments bias reward learning toward intrinsic signals (internal state representations) over extrinsic signals (external reward delivery). An open question is the neurobiological substrate of basic need satisfaction: whether thwarted autonomy generates a neural threat response (amygdala/insula activation analogous to social exclusion) or operates via a distinct unfulfilled-expectancy system.

real_life_examples: Employees with flexible work arrangements outperforming closely monitored ones
Students choosing their own essay topics producing better work
Athletes citing love of sport sustaining training through adversity
SDT-based patient-centred care improving diabetes self-management

title: Flow State
brain_region: Prefrontal Cortex / Striatum / Anterior Cingulate Cortex

summary.beginner: Flow is the experience of complete absorption in a challenging activity — time disappears, self-consciousness vanishes, and performance peaks effortlessly. The analogy: flow is like being in a perfect current that carries you precisely as fast as you paddle. The concrete example: a programmer who sits down to fix a bug and looks up to find four hours have passed without noticing — the task consumed all attentional resources so completely that time perception was suspended.
summary.university: Csikszentmihalyi (1990) identified flow as arising when challenge level matches skill level at a high threshold: too easy → boredom; too hard → anxiety; matched at high level → flow. Eight components include challenge-skill balance, action-awareness merging, clear goals, unambiguous feedback, concentration, sense of control, loss of self-consciousness, and time transformation. The transient hypofrontality hypothesis (Dietrich, 2004) proposes flow involves reduced dlPFC self-monitoring, explaining loss of self-consciousness. Within positive psychology and performance science, flow is studied as peak human functioning.
summary.research: EEG during flow (operationalised via challenge-skill balance in video games) shows increased alpha/theta oscillations in PFC (consistent with reduced executive self-monitoring) and increased gamma in task-relevant sensorimotor cortex (consistent with focused processing). De Manzano et al. (2010) confirmed cardiovascular and EEG signatures of flow in pianists performing at the edge of skill, providing objective validation. Computationally, flow may represent an optimal Bayesian inference state: high-precision prediction with low error, enabling effortless forward modelling without need for corrective attention. An open question is whether flow is a distinct brain state or simply the subjective experience of high-efficiency processing — the absence of measurable flow-specific neural signatures independent of task performance makes this difficult to resolve.

real_life_examples: A surgeon in the middle of a complex procedure unaware of passing time
A programmer solving a complex bug and looking up to find 4 hours have passed
Musicians improvising in a jazz ensemble reaching collective flow
Rock climbers reporting flow on challenging routes requiring full concentration

title: Delay Discounting

summary.beginner: Delay discounting is the brain's bias toward immediate rewards over larger future ones — we hyperbolically devalue anything we have to wait for. The analogy: a £50 note today feels more valuable than a £100 cheque dated six months from now, even though waiting doubles the money. The concrete example: smokers who genuinely intend to quit tomorrow but choose to smoke today — the immediate craving is discounted forward, while the future quitting is discounted back.
summary.university: Delay discounting follows a hyperbolic function V = A/(1+kD) — not the exponential V = A·e^(−kD) of rational normative models — meaning subjective value drops steeply for short delays then levels off, producing preference reversals as the smaller-sooner option approaches. Higher k (steeper discounting) is a transdiagnostic risk factor across addiction, ADHD, obesity, and depression. The neural substrate involves two systems: limbic/striatal (immediacy bias, mesolimbic dopamine) and dlPFC-parietal (patience via prospective simulation of future value). Within behavioural economics and clinical psychology, delay discounting is a measurable index of impulsivity.
summary.research: Laibson (1997) formalised hyperbolic discounting in economic theory as quasi-hyperbolic (β,δ) preferences, explaining present bias without abandoning intertemporal utility theory. McClure et al. (2004) used fMRI to show β-system (limbic/striatal) activity for immediate rewards and δ-system (lateral PFC-parietal) activity for both immediate and delayed rewards — though this dual-system interpretation has been questioned by single-system accounts. An open question is whether delay discounting is modifiable via intervention: cognitive training, mindfulness, and future self-continuity interventions show small-to-moderate effects on k, but no intervention reliably produces lasting decreases in impulsivity in clinical populations.

triggers: Choice between immediate and delayed rewards
real_life_examples: Choosing fast food over a meal prep plan because hunger is immediate
Spending a bonus instead of investing due to present bias
Smokers knowing the future harm but unable to delay current gratification
Children's marshmallow test performance predicting SAT scores 14 years later

title: Learned Helplessness

summary.beginner: Learned helplessness is the psychological shutdown that occurs after repeated exposure to uncontrollable negative events — the organism stops trying, even when escape becomes possible, because it has 'learned' that its actions don't matter. The analogy: it's like a circuit breaker that trips and won't reset — the animal has updated its model of the world to 'nothing I do changes the outcome.' The concrete example: students who experience chronic academic failure stop attempting problems, even on new material well within their ability.
summary.university: Seligman & Maier (1967) demonstrated learned helplessness in dogs exposed to inescapable shock: subsequent exposure to escapable shock produced failure to escape — unlike controls. In humans, exposure to uncontrollable outcomes produces motivational deficits (reduced instrumental responding), cognitive deficits (impaired ability to learn when control is available), and emotional deficits (depression, anxiety). Attribution style moderates generalisation: internal, stable, global failure attributions ('I'm always like this in everything') produce the most severe and generalised helplessness. Within clinical psychology, learned helplessness is a core model of depression.
summary.research: Maier & Seligman's (2016) major revision reframed the mechanism: passive resignation to inescapable threat is the brain's default response; controllability actively suppresses this passivity via a ventral mPFC→dorsal raphe serotonin circuit. This inverts the original model — helplessness is not learned, it is the default, and coping must be actively learned. Computationally, controllability is detected when action-outcome contingency exceeds a threshold — formalised as covariance between responses and outcomes in an agent's learned world model. An open question is whether antidepressants relieve depression by restoring this vmPFC→raphe controllability signal (suggesting mPFC strengthening as a treatment target), or whether serotonergic effects on mood are upstream of controllability learning.

real_life_examples: Seligman's dogs refusing to jump the barrier even when shock was escapable
Students in chronic academic failure giving up studying entirely
Long-term unemployed individuals stopping job applications
Prisoners in total institutions losing initiative after prolonged control removal

title: False Memory

summary.beginner: False memories are vivid, confident recollections of events that never happened. Memory is not a video recording — it is a reconstruction, and every retrieval is an act of creative rebuilding that can introduce errors. The analogy: memory is less like a photograph and more like a painting that gets retouched every time it's displayed. The concrete example: eyewitnesses to crimes regularly misidentify innocent suspects with complete confidence — courts have exonerated hundreds of wrongfully convicted people based on this failure.
summary.university: Loftus & Palmer (1974) demonstrated that post-event misinformation ('smashed' vs. 'hit') altered both speed estimates and introduced false memories of broken glass — a finding foundational to eyewitness testimony research. The DRM paradigm (Deese-Roediger-McDermott) reliably produces false recall of 'critical lure' words from semantically related lists via spreading activation in hippocampal-neocortical networks. Source monitoring errors — confusion between internally imagined and externally experienced events — are the cognitive mechanism. Within cognitive psychology and legal psychology, false memory is studied as memory distortion and eyewitness reliability.
summary.research: Loftus & Pickrell (1995) implanted false childhood memories (being lost in a shopping mall) in ~25% of participants using suggestion alone — demonstrating that entire autobiographical episodes can be fabricated. Computationally, false memories arise from pattern completion in CA3: when a retrieval cue activates related content, the associative network converges on a plausible but inaccurate reconstruction weighted by prior knowledge schemas. An open question is whether false memories and true memories are neurally distinguishable — Okado & Stark (2005) identified differences in hippocampal and PFC BOLD during encoding that predicted subsequent true versus false recall, suggesting encoding differences exist, but real-time detection remains infeasible.

real_life_examples: Eyewitnesses to crimes confidently misidentifying innocent suspects
Loftus implanting detailed false childhood memories in 25% of participants
Therapist-implanted 'repressed memories' leading to wrongful abuse convictions
Flashbulb memories (9/11) containing systematic reconstruction errors

title: Memory Reconsolidation

summary.beginner: Every time you retrieve a memory, it briefly becomes unstable and can be modified before being stored again — a process called reconsolidation. The analogy: memory is like a document that becomes editable each time you open it, and if someone changes it while it's open, the changed version is saved. The concrete example: this is the basis of trauma therapy techniques where a patient retrieves a fear memory under safe conditions, allowing the emotional charge to be updated before re-storage.
summary.university: Nader, Schafe & LeDoux (2000) demonstrated in rats that reactivating a consolidated fear memory returns it to a labile (protein-synthesis-dependent) state: infusing anisomycin into the BLA within 6 hours of reactivation prevents re-storage, erasing the memory. In humans, reactivation followed by a reconsolidation window (6 hours) allows new information to update the memory trace. BLA AMPA receptor trafficking, PKA, and ERK signalling cascades are required for reconsolidation. Within memory neuroscience and clinical psychology, reconsolidation is studied for its therapeutic potential in PTSD and addiction.
summary.research: Kindt et al. (2009) demonstrated in humans that propranolol (β-adrenergic blocker) administered during memory reactivation attenuated fear expression to the CS one day later without affecting declarative memory — consistent with selective emotional reconsolidation blockade. Computationally, reconsolidation updates a generative model: reactivation generates a prediction, and prediction error (something unexpected during recall) triggers a model update proportional to error magnitude. An open question is why reconsolidation fails under some conditions (very strong memories, very old memories, absence of prediction error) — the boundary condition framework (Forcato et al.) is descriptively useful but lacks a unifying mechanistic account of when reconsolidation is versus is not engaged.

real_life_examples: PTSD therapy using propranolol during memory recall to reduce fear charge
Misremembering a story's details after discussing it with a friend
Reconsolidation explaining why memories feel different after therapy
Sports psychology using imagery reconsolidation to update performance memories

title: Spacing Effect

summary.beginner: The spacing effect is the finding that spreading study sessions over time produces dramatically better long-term memory than cramming the same total study time into one session. The analogy: watering a plant every few days for a month is more effective than drowning it in one session. The concrete example: students who use spaced repetition flashcard software (like Anki) consistently outperform students who read their notes repeatedly, even with less total study time.
summary.university: Ebbinghaus (1885) first systematically documented the forgetting curve and its companion: distributed practice produces superior retention. The desirable difficulties framework (Bjork, 1994) explains why: spaced retrieval requires greater effortful processing each time (target memory has partially decayed), driving deeper encoding. Optimal spacing follows an expanding schedule proportional to the retention interval (initial short spacing, then progressively longer). Within cognitive psychology and educational science, this is one of the most replicated and practically impactful findings in memory research.
summary.research: Cepeda et al.'s (2008) large meta-analysis of spacing studies established the optimal study-to-test ratio: spacing ~10–20% of the retention interval maximises recall at test. Neurally, each spaced retrieval activates hippocampal-prefrontal reinstatement circuits; accumulation across sessions drives progressive neocortical consolidation proportional to retrieval count. Computationally, the spacing effect is formalised by the Retrievability model (R-MCMC) and the New Theory of Disuse (Bjork & Bjork, 1992), where retrieval strength increases and storage strength consolidates with each spaced retrieval event. An open question is whether interleaving different topics during spacing (interleaved practice) confers additional benefits beyond spacing alone — evidence suggests it does for discrimination learning, but the neural mechanism of interleaving-specific benefits is unclear.

real_life_examples: Medical students using Anki for spaced repetition outperforming traditional reading
Language learners reviewing vocabulary with expanding interval flashcards
All-night cramming producing worse exam performance one week later than spaced study
Physical skills practiced across days showing better retention than massed same-day sessions

title: Chunking

summary.beginner: Chunking is the process of grouping individual pieces of information into meaningful units, dramatically expanding effective memory capacity. The analogy: phone numbers — 07700900123 — are meaningless strings of digits, but grouped as 077-009-00123, they become three manageable chunks. The concrete example: expert chess players can glance at a mid-game board for 5 seconds and reproduce every piece — not by memorising 32 positions, but by recognising ~5–7 familiar tactical patterns (chunks).
summary.university: Miller's (1956) 'magical number 7' established chunking as the strategy by which humans circumvent working memory limits. Chase & Simon (1973) demonstrated that chess expertise relies on a long-term memory store of ~50,000–100,000 chunk patterns enabling rapid board recognition — the expertise is in the chunks, not raw cognitive capacity. Chunking depends on long-term memory schemas providing grouping structure; dlPFC maintains chunk boundaries during active use. Within cognitive psychology, chunking explains expertise effects across music, programming, and language, and underlies sequence learning in basal ganglia.
summary.research: Basal ganglia (specifically dorsal striatum) implement sequential action chunking: as sequences are learned, striatal activity shifts from item-by-item to start/stop boundary markers of whole chunks, reducing the computational cost of sequence execution (Graybiel, 1998; Jin et al., 2014). Computationally, chunking implements a hierarchical compression: sequences are encoded as programmes with chunk-level calls, analogous to function definitions in programming that reduce representational cost. An open question is how chunk formation is controlled — whether it is driven by temporal contiguity, statistical co-occurrence, or explicit goal structure — and whether the same chunking mechanism operates in both motor sequences and conceptual knowledge hierarchies.

real_life_examples: Expert chess players recalling entire board positions after 5 seconds
Phone numbers grouped in groups of 3 for easier memory
Experienced programmers reading code as functional blocks rather than individual characters
Musicians reading sheet music as phrases rather than individual notes

title: Priming

summary.beginner: Priming is the way prior exposure to a stimulus influences how you respond to a later, related stimulus — usually below conscious awareness. The analogy: priming is like pre-loading a search engine's autocomplete — exposure to 'doctor' makes 'nurse' come to mind faster and with less effort. The concrete example: hospital environments designed with calm colours and natural imagery demonstrably reduce patients' pain ratings — the environment primes a calmer physiological and cognitive state.
summary.university: Priming is classified into: perceptual priming (faster processing of previously encountered stimuli via repetition suppression in relevant sensory cortices), semantic priming (spreading activation through associative memory networks — reduced N400 ERP amplitude for related word pairs), and conceptual priming. Tulving & Schacter's (1990) perceptual representation system (PRS) is a dedicated implicit memory system dissociable from hippocampus-dependent episodic memory — PRS priming survives amnesia that abolishes explicit recall. This falls within cognitive neuroscience and implicit memory research.
summary.research: Meyer & Schvaneveldt (1971) established semantic priming using lexical decision tasks — reaction times to target words are faster when preceded by semantically related primes, indexed by reduced N400. Computationally, semantic priming implements spreading activation in a semantic network: prime activation propagates to related nodes proportional to associative strength, pre-activating targets. An open question is whether behavioural priming (e.g., Bargh et al.'s elderly word prime slowing walking speed) replicates reliably — large direct replications failed, casting doubt on automatic motor priming from abstract word meaning, while semantic and perceptual priming remain robustly replicable.

real_life_examples: Seeing fast food logos making unhealthy food seem more appealing
Reading 'bank' in a financial context making 'money' easier to recognise
Weapon primes increasing aggressive word completions in laboratory studies
Environmental art in hospitals priming calm vs. anxiety states

title: Tip-of-the-Tongue Phenomenon
brain_region: Prefrontal Cortex / Temporal Cortex / Angular Gyrus

summary.beginner: The tip-of-the-tongue (TOT) state is the frustrating experience of knowing you know a word or name but being unable to retrieve it — you can sense its length, first letter, even rhymes, but the word itself won't come. The analogy: it's like knowing a file is on your hard drive but the search function refuses to return it. A concrete everyday example: confidently knowing a film actor's face and filmography but being completely unable to recall their name.
summary.university: Brown & McNeill (1966) systematically studied TOT states induced by rare word definitions: participants in TOT could often report first letter, syllable count, and phonologically similar words, demonstrating partial but incomplete lexical access. Burke et al.'s (1991) transmission deficit model proposes TOTs reflect weak priming of phonological representations from lemma (semantic/syntactic) level — the word's meaning is accessible but its sound form (lexeme) is insufficiently primed. Within psycholinguistics and memory research, TOT is studied as a window into the staged architecture of lexical retrieval.
summary.research: fMRI during TOT (versus Know versus Don't Know states) shows elevated ACC/anterior frontal monitoring activity and reduced left temporal (phonological production) activation — consistent with heightened monitoring of a lexical retrieval failure. Computationally, TOT reflects an asymmetry in a two-stage model: lemma retrieval succeeds (semantic access is complete), but phonological encoding fails — modelled as a stochastic threshold that the target's phonological node fails to exceed despite partial activation. An open question is whether attempting to retrieve the blocked word during TOT helps or hurts: the 'ugly sister' effect (Rastle & Burke, 1996) suggests competing near-homophones block target retrieval, meaning the most effective strategy may be distraction rather than continued search — but individual and situational moderators of this effect are poorly understood.

real_life_examples: Knowing an actor's face perfectly but being unable to recall their name
Struggling to recall a technical term you use every few months
Second-language speakers having more TOTs for L2 words due to weaker phonological links
Tip-of-the-tongue for country capitals despite confidently knowing them

title: Dual Process Theory (System 1 & 2)

summary.beginner: Dual process theory proposes that the mind runs two decision systems: System 1 is fast, automatic, and emotional — like immediately recognising a friend's face; System 2 is slow, deliberate, and effortful — like solving 17 × 34 in your head. The analogy: System 1 is driving on a familiar road on autopilot; System 2 is navigating an unfamiliar city at night with a map. A concrete example: a doctor instantly recognising a rare condition from experience (S1 pattern recognition), then ordering confirmatory tests (S2 deliberation).
summary.university: Kahneman's (2011) popularisation of dual-process theory synthesised research from Evans, Stanovich, and Sloman. System 1 relies on associative pattern-matching, heuristics, and learned intuitions; System 2 uses rule-based sequential reasoning and logical operations. Neurally, S1 processes map to amygdala, striatum, and insula (fast, affective, automatic) while S2 maps to dlPFC, ACC, and lateral parietal cortex (slow, controlled). Within cognitive psychology and behavioural economics, dual-process accounts explain why intelligent people fall for biases (S1 dominates under cognitive load and time pressure).
summary.research: Evans' default interventionist model (2006) proposes that S2 functions as an optional corrective override of S1 defaults — it intervenes selectively when ACC conflict signals flag S1 outputs as requiring scrutiny. Computationally, dual-process architectures are formalised in hybrid cognitive models (e.g., ACT-R) where a fast associative module generates candidate responses and a slower deliberative module selects and monitors them. An open question is whether two discrete systems actually exist or whether 'dual process' describes a continuum of automaticity — Keren & Schul (2009) and Melnikoff & Bargh (2018) argue the dichotomy is a theoretical convenience, and that automaticity is graded rather than categorical.

real_life_examples: Immediately knowing 2+2=4 (S1) vs. calculating 17×23 requiring paper (S2)
A doctor's instant diagnosis from pattern recognition (S1) verified by test ordering (S2)
Grabbing a familiar snack without thinking (S1) vs. reading nutrition labels (S2)
Financial advisors reverting to S1 heuristics under time pressure

title: Prospect Theory

summary.beginner: Prospect Theory describes how people actually make decisions under risk — and it's very different from how economists assumed people would. Rather than calculating expected value, people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point, and losses feel about twice as bad as equivalent gains feel good. The analogy: your emotional response to financial news isn't about how much you have — it's about whether you gained or lost from yesterday. A concrete example: a trader who has made £10,000 this year but lost £2,000 last week feels net miserable — the recent loss dominates despite the net gain.
summary.university: Kahneman & Tversky (1979, 1992) developed Prospect Theory as a descriptive alternative to expected utility theory. Its components: (1) a reference-dependent value function v(x) — concave for gains (diminishing sensitivity), convex and steeper for losses (loss aversion); (2) a probability weighting function w(p) — overweighting small probabilities, underweighting medium-to-large ones. The fourfold pattern results: risk-aversion for probable gains, risk-seeking for probable losses, risk-seeking for improbable gains, risk-aversion for improbable losses. In neuroeconomics, vmPFC encodes reference-adjusted value while insula/amygdala track loss magnitude.
summary.research: Tom et al. (2007) mapped Prospect Theory parameters onto neural substrates: the ratio of BOLD response slopes for losses versus gains in ventral striatum/vmPFC predicts individual λ (loss aversion coefficient), providing a neural utility function measurable by fMRI. Cumulative Prospect Theory (Tversky & Kahneman, 1992) extended the original model by applying probability weighting to cumulative rather than individual probabilities, resolving stochastic dominance violations. An open question is whether Prospect Theory's parameters (α, β, λ, γ) are stable individual traits or highly context-dependent — evidence shows loss aversion varies substantially across domains (financial vs. social vs. health), suggesting domain-specific reference systems rather than a single universal value function.

real_life_examples: Buying lottery tickets despite negative expected value (overweighting small win probability)
Insurance purchase for small losses despite unfavourable expected value
Selling winning investments too early but holding losers too long (disposition effect)
Negotiators anchoring to a reference point and treating concessions as losses

title: Bounded Rationality

summary.beginner: Bounded rationality is the recognition that real decision-makers are not perfect calculators — they have limited information, limited time, and finite cognitive capacity, so they make decisions that are 'good enough' within these real constraints. The analogy: a chess grandmaster doesn't compute every possible move — they use trained pattern recognition to find a very good move quickly, because perfect computation would take centuries. A concrete example: a doctor choosing the most likely diagnosis from pattern recognition rather than methodically computing probabilities for every disease that matches the symptoms.
summary.university: Herbert Simon (1955, 1956) introduced bounded rationality as an alternative to the omniscient rational agent of neoclassical economics. Real agents search sequentially through a problem space and accept the first option exceeding an aspiration level — satisficing — rather than optimising. Ecological rationality (Gigerenzer) extends this: heuristics are well-adapted to real-world statistical environments and often outperform complex models. ACC monitors aspiration-level attainment; anterior PFC manages sequential search termination decisions. Within decision science and behavioural economics, bounded rationality provides the descriptive foundation for human judgment.
summary.research: Gigerenzer & Goldstein's (1996) Take-the-Best heuristic — using only the single most valid cue and ignoring all others — outperforms multiple regression on out-of-sample prediction by avoiding overfitting, demonstrating that information frugality can be an advantage. Lieder & Griffiths (2020) formalise bounded rationality as resource-rational analysis: optimal cognition under computational cost constraints, producing simple heuristics as the Pareto-optimal solution to the accuracy-cost tradeoff. An open question is whether the environment that shaped human heuristics (ancestral ecology) is similar enough to modern environments for ecological optimality claims to hold — Kahneman's counter-argument is that modern financial, medical, and statistical decision environments are evolutionarily novel and therefore systematically mismatch our evolved heuristics.

real_life_examples: Choosing the first restaurant on a street that looks acceptable rather than comparing all options
Hiring managers using gut-feel shortcuts rather than systematic evaluation
Consumer purchasing stopping search when a product meets minimum requirements
Emergency room triage using simple classification trees to outperform complex scoring systems

title: Satisficing

summary.beginner: Satisficing (satisfying + sufficing) is the decision strategy of choosing the first option that meets your minimum requirements, rather than searching for the absolute best. The analogy: it's the difference between searching for a parking spot until you find the very closest one (maximising) versus pulling into the first acceptable spot you find (satisficing). A concrete example: choosing the first apartment that meets your budget, location, and size criteria — rather than comparing every available apartment in the city, exhausting yourself, and potentially missing out by waiting for something better.
summary.university: Simon (1956) coined satisficing as the empirically observed decision rule of bounded rational agents: set an aspiration level, search sequentially, and accept the first option that exceeds it. Barry Schwartz's (2004) Paradox of Choice documented that maximisers — people who seek the best rather than good enough — report lower wellbeing, higher regret, and more social comparison than satisficers, despite sometimes making objectively better choices. Neurally, orbitofrontal cortex supports ongoing comparative value computation in maximising; anterior PFC monitors aspiration-threshold attainment in satisficing. Within decision science, satisficing is a core component of bounded rationality theory.
summary.research: Computationally, satisficing implements a threshold-stopping rule in sequential search: the agent accepts option x if u(x) ≥ θ, where θ is the aspiration level, reducing the search problem to a one-parameter optimisation. Iyengar & Lepper's (2000) jam study showed 10× lower purchase rate with 24 options versus 6 — satisficing with limited options outperforms maximising with abundant ones in real-world purchasing behaviour. An open question is whether aspiration levels are dynamically updated during search (rational satisficing) or remain fixed (rigid threshold), and how disappointment about foregone options enters the aspiration-adjustment process — the theory predicts updating but empirical evidence is mixed.

real_life_examples: Picking the first apartment that meets your budget, location, and size criteria
Buying the recommended laptop rather than researching 50 models
Maximisers taking longer to choose jobs and being less happy with them afterward
'Good enough' menus reducing decision fatigue in restaurant design

title: Choice Overload

summary.beginner: Choice overload is the paradoxical finding that having too many options makes people less likely to choose at all — and less satisfied when they do. The analogy: standing in front of a vending machine with 200 items feels less satisfying than one with 12 clear options. The concrete example: Iyengar & Lepper's jam study — a display of 24 jams drew more people in, but produced only 1/10 the purchase rate of a display of just 6 jams.
summary.university: Iyengar & Lepper (2000) demonstrated the paradox of choice: more options increase exploration but reduce commitment and satisfaction. Choice overload is mediated by: increased difficulty of comparison, higher opportunity cost salience (imagining all the unchosen options), and higher standards (more options means the best option should theoretically be better, raising the comparison bar). Neurally, large assortments trigger ACC conflict signals that discourage engagement; vmPFC value signals degrade as option count increases. Within consumer psychology and decision science, choice overload explains optimum assortment strategies.
summary.research: Scheibehenne et al.'s (2010) meta-analysis of 50 choice overload studies found a mean effect near zero, identifying moderators: choice overload is most reliable when consumers lack clear preferences, options are similar, and no search aids are provided. Computationally, choice overload can be modelled as decision noise increasing with option count: as comparisons proliferate, value signals become less distinguishable from noise, reducing confidence in any choice and increasing post-choice regret. An open question is whether choice overload is a supply-side problem (too many options presented) or a demand-side problem (consumers with unclear preferences who need fewer options to decide) — evidence suggests both moderators interact, with consumer expertise being the critical variable.

real_life_examples: Netflix's 'what to watch' paradox — vast catalogue but paralysis choosing
Iyengar's 24-jam vs. 6-jam study showing 10× lower sales with more options
401(k) plan participation declining as investment fund options increase
Simplified menus (5 items) in restaurants increasing customer satisfaction
